$comment: Glossary terms used in existential explanations not yet in data/glossary.json. Project author reviews and merges. Checked against glossary.json v2 (elision, definite article, indefinite article already present; existential construction and definiteness are not).

existential construction.short: Saying that something exists or is present with esserci: c'è (one thing) and ci sono (more than one). C'è un bar; ci sono due bar.
existential construction.long: The existential construction states that something is there, rather than saying where a known thing is. Italian builds it on esserci (ci + essere) and agrees it in number with the thing that exists: singular c'è, plural ci sono, past c'era / c'erano / c'è stato. It contrasts with plain essere, which locates a known, definite thing (il libro è sul tavolo), and with ecco, which presents something in the moment (ecco il libro).
existential construction.needed_for: Nearly every existential explanation names the construction as the four-beat term; explanations use the bare word 'existential' repeatedly, so an alias from 'existential' to this entry would let the renderer wrap it.

definiteness.short: Whether a noun is presented as new/indefinite (un libro) or already known/definite (il libro). It is the main signal for c'è vs è.
definiteness.long: Definiteness is the difference between introducing something new (indefinite: un, una) and referring to something already known (definite: il, la). It drives the c'è-vs-essere choice: a new, indefinite thing is introduced with the existential c'è (c'è un libro sul tavolo), while a known, definite thing is located with plain essere (il libro è sul tavolo). The indefinite and definite articles are the surface clue to it.
definiteness.needed_for: The vs_essere explanations turn on new-and-indefinite versus known-and-definite; 'definite article' and 'indefinite article' exist in the glossary but the underlying property does not.
